scriptFull:
To understand why this story matters — why this attack sent emergency memos racing through hospital networks and forced paramedics to revert to radio calls for cardiac patients — you need to understand who Stryker is.
Fifty-six thousand employees. Sixty-one countries. Twenty-five billion dollars in annual revenue. Their products include the surgical robots used in hip replacements, the powered stretchers in emergency rooms, the orthopedic implants that restore mobility to patients who had none, and LifeNet — a cardiac transmission platform that lets paramedics beam a patient's electrocardiogram to a waiting emergency physician before the ambulance has even reached the hospital door.
Almost every hospital in the United States that performs surgery sources equipment through Stryker. They are not background. They are the foundation.
On the morning of the attack, all of it went dark at once.
More than five thousand employees at Stryker's largest hub outside the United States were sent home. Manufacturing lines stood motionless. Staff were communicating through WhatsApp on personal phones because every official channel had been destroyed. When a journalist called the company's main US headquarters, the voicemail said simply: 'We are currently experiencing a building emergency. Please try your call again later.'
In Maryland, the state's emergency medical services office sent a memo to paramedics across the state. Stryker's LifeNet cardiac platform was unavailable. If they could not transmit a twelve-lead ECG to a receiving hospital, they should initiate radio consultation and describe the findings verbally.
The implication was immediate: cardiac patients in ambulances, their readings described over radio rather than transmitted automatically — because the system that was supposed to do it was offline.
To understand how this was possible, you need to understand a tool most people have never heard of — and a technique that leaves almost no fingerprints.

Almost every hospital in the United States that performs surgery sources equipment through Stryker. They are not background. They are the foundation.

scenes.prompt: Cinematic wide shot of a sterile, modern hospital hallway. The lights are flickering and dying. Computer monitors at the nursing station are black, showing no data. The atmosphere is cold and ominous. High contrast, deep shadows, blue and grey color palette. Photorealistic, 8k, highly detailed.
scenes.motion: Slow zoom into a dead monitor screen, ambient light flickers and dies out.

scenes.script: More than five thousand employees at Stryker's largest hub outside the United States were sent home. Manufacturing lines stood motionless.
Staff were communicating through WhatsApp on personal phones because every official channel had been destroyed.

The lighting is dim, coming only from the phone screen. Photorealistic, cinematic, shallow depth of field.
scenes.motion: Camera drifts slowly across the screen as messages scroll by.
